replies.msg:
It’s such an inaccurate thing to say that analytic number theory is a “dead field” or “dying field”. It’s an extremely active field but it’s fair to say innovation and new ideas are much needed in this field.

For the biggest breakthrough in analtyic number theory this year, think it might be the statistical result of Collatz conjecture by Terence Tao (again)?
https://www.quantamagazine.org/mathematician-terence-tao-and-the-collatz-conjecture-20191211/

replies.msg: That brings the interesting question: how should one define improper integrals like \int_{-infty}^{\infty} f(x)dx?

quote.msg: Thats not true. The second integral does not exist cuz it equals \lim_{a -> -\infty} a^2 which does not exist.
Oh no, it’s zero i misread the question im sorry lol
That brings the interesting question: how should one define improper integrals like \int_{-infty}^{\infty} f(x)dx?

replies.msg:
a classical way is the following:
for a real number d, define
\int_d^infinity f(x) dx = \lim_{a -> infinity} \int_d^a f(x) dx
and define \int_{-infinity}^d f(x) dx similarly,
then, if there is a "cutting point" c such that
\int_c^infinity f(x) dx and \int_{-infinity}^c f(x) dx are finite numbers
we define \int_R f(x) dx be the sum of them,
and then you can show that if such point exists, then any point is also a "cutting point" and the value of the sum remains unchanged.
